     Sources: src/demos/, src/content/components/, src/data/features.ts -->

# Header

A sticky app bar that hides as you scroll down and slides back when you scroll up — plus a section header that lifts when it docks. Pure CSS, driven by container scroll-state queries.

A header does two things while you scroll: it can get **out of the way**, and it can **announce
when it's docked**. Container scroll-state queries let a sticky element
react to how its scroller is moving, with no scroll listeners, observers, or state.

## Hide on scroll down, reveal on scroll up

```html
<div class="overflow-hidden isolate rounded-xl border border-border">
  <div class="h-80 w-full max-w-sm overflow-y-auto [container-type:scroll-state]">
    <header
      class="sticky top-0 z-10 flex items-center rounded-t-xl gap-3 border-b border-border bg-background/85 px-4 py-3 backdrop-blur transition-[translate] duration-300 ease-out [@container_scroll-state(scrolled:block-end)]:-translate-y-full"
    >
      <span class="text-sm font-semibold tracking-tight">
        unscripted<span class="text-muted-foreground">/ui</span>
      </span>
      <nav class="ml-auto flex items-center gap-4 text-sm text-muted-foreground">
        <a href="#" class="transition-colors hover:text-foreground">Docs</a>
        <a href="#" class="transition-colors hover:text-foreground">Components</a>
      </nav>
    </header>

    <div class="space-y-4 p-4 text-sm/6 text-muted-foreground">
      <p class="font-medium text-foreground">Scroll down — the bar slides away. Scroll up — it comes right back.</p>
      <p>No scroll listener, no <code class="font-mono text-xs">requestAnimationFrame</code>, no state. The bar is a plain sticky element; a container scroll-state query does the hiding.</p>
      <p>The trick is a single conditional: while the container was last scrolled toward its block-end, the bar translates up out of view. Reverse direction and the query stops matching, so it returns.</p>
      <p>Because it reads scroll <em>direction</em> rather than position, the bar reappears the instant you scroll up — giving you the whole viewport back until you want the chrome again.</p>
      <p>This is the pattern shipped by news sites and docs apps everywhere, usually built with a few dozen lines of JavaScript and a resize observer. Here it's four declarations.</p>
      <p>Everything degrades cleanly: where scroll-state queries aren't supported yet, you keep an ordinary always-visible sticky header.</p>
      <p>Keep scrolling to feel it settle, then flick back up. The motion respects <code class="font-mono text-xs">prefers-reduced-motion</code> automatically.</p>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

Scroll down and the bar slides up out of view; the instant you scroll back up, it returns. Because
it reads scroll *direction* rather than position, the bar responds to which way you're going, not
how far down the page you are.

- The scroll container is marked `[container-type:scroll-state]`, which lets its descendants query
  its scroll state. The bar itself is a plain `sticky top-0` element.
- `@container scroll-state(scrolled: block-end)` matches while the container was last scrolled
  *down*, and applies `translate: 0 -100%` to slide the bar away; scroll up and the query stops
  matching, so it returns to `0`. In Tailwind that whole conditional is one utility:
  `[@container_scroll-state(scrolled:block-end)]:-translate-y-full`.
- The demo scrolls inside its own bounded box on purpose. A `scrolled` query reads the nearest
  scroll-container ancestor, so a self-contained region keeps each example independent. In a real
  layout you'd mark the element that actually scrolls, often the document root.

## Elevate when it docks

```html
<div class="h-80 w-full max-w-sm overflow-y-auto rounded-xl border border-border">
  <div class="space-y-3 p-4 text-sm/6 text-muted-foreground">
    <p class="font-medium text-foreground">Keep scrolling — the section header docks, then lifts.</p>
    <p>The bar sits flat in the flow of the page. The moment it pins to the top edge it gains a shadow, separating it from the content sliding underneath.</p>
  </div>

  <header class="sticky -top-px z-10 [container-type:scroll-state]">
    <div
      class="flex items-center justify-between border-y border-border bg-background/85 px-4 py-2.5 backdrop-blur transition-shadow duration-200 [@container_scroll-state(stuck:top)]:shadow-lg"
    >
      <span class="text-sm font-semibold tracking-tight">Section title</span>
      <span class="font-mono text-xs text-muted-foreground">stuck: top</span>
    </div>
  </header>

  <div class="space-y-4 p-4 text-sm/6 text-muted-foreground">
    <p>Here <code class="font-mono text-xs">container-type: scroll-state</code> lives on the sticky element itself, and a query asks whether it's currently stuck.</p>
    <p>Scroll-state queries can only style the container's descendants, so the shadow is painted on an inner bar rather than the <code class="font-mono text-xs">&lt;header&gt;</code> node.</p>
    <p>It's the classic "flat until it sticks, then it elevates" affordance — the one that normally needs an IntersectionObserver sentinel to detect the pinned state.</p>
    <p>No sentinel, no observer, no scroll handler. The browser tracks stickiness and flips the query for you.</p>
    <p>Scroll back to the top and the shadow fades out as the header rejoins the flow.</p>
    <p>Because it's a real sticky element, the header keeps its landmark, its links, and its place in the tab order the entire time.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

A section header that sits flat in the flow, then lifts with a shadow the moment it pins to the top
edge. The shadow is the cue that content is now scrolling beneath it.

- Here `container-type: scroll-state` goes on the sticky element itself, and
  `@container scroll-state(stuck: top)` matches once it's pinned to the top edge.
- Scroll-state queries only style a container's *descendants*, so the shadow lives on an inner bar
  inside the `<header>` rather than on the header node.
- It's the "flat until it sticks, then it lifts" affordance you'd normally build with an
  `IntersectionObserver` sentinel. Here it's one utility and zero JavaScript.

## Accessibility

The bar moves only *visually*. It stays in the DOM, in the tab order, and reachable by
keyboard and screen reader the whole time. Nothing is inserted or removed on scroll, so focus and
announcements stay stable. Mark the `<header>` up as a `banner`/`navigation` landmark as you
normally would.

## Reduced motion

The global `prefers-reduced-motion` rule collapses the slide and the shadow fade to an instant
change. The bar still hides and reveals, it just doesn't travel.

## Leading-edge features used (and what happens without them)

- **Scroll-state query: scrolled (direction)** ([MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Guides/Conditional_rules/Container_scroll-state_queries))
  Without it: The header stays permanently visible — an ordinary sticky header. Nothing is hidden and no content is lost.
- **Scroll-state query: stuck** ([MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Guides/Conditional_rules/Container_scroll-state_queries))
  Without it: The docked header simply does not gain its elevation shadow.
